summary: Fowler argues that Specification By Example—using concrete tests as specifications—is a powerful and practical alternative to formal pre/post conditions and traditional requirements documents. While examples don't cover all cases like general specifications, they're much easier to create, especially for non-technical stakeholders. The approach provides a valuable double-check mechanism by expressing requirements differently in tests versus code. Fowler acknowledges this technique is incomplete and must be combined with other practices like conversation and Domain Driven Design, but sees this incompleteness as a feature that prevents teams from thinking communication is 'done' after writing specs.
key_points: Examples are often easier to write than formal pre/post conditions, especially for non-programmers, even though they don't cover all cases
TDD involves a valuable double-check principle—saying things twice in different ways (code and tests) increases error detection
Specification By Example makes verification easy compared to formal methods, being 'less valuable in theory but more valuable in practice'
The incompleteness of example-based specs is actually beneficial because it forces teams to maintain ongoing communication rather than treating requirements as 'done'
This approach only works when both sides are collaborating rather than fighting—examples trigger abstractions while keeping them grounded
money_quotes: Rather like if someone offered me a million dollars to hike up a mountain. I may say that the main purpose of the hike is the enjoyment of nature, but the side-effect to my wallet is hardly trivial.
I've found that in many situations it's as hard to write the pre and post conditions as it is to write the solution.
They make a very strong point of saying things Once and Only Once, but in fact they always say things twice: once in the code and once in the tests.
Another case of Specification By Example being less valuable in theory but more valuable in practice.
My flippant answer to this is that if you are concerned about this then the issue of tests versus Design by Contract is the least of your worries.
One of the most dangerous things about a traditional requirements specification is when people think that once they've written it they are done communicating.
Specification By Example is a powerful tool, perhaps my most used tool, but never my only tool.
key_insight: Specification By Example succeeds not despite its incompleteness but partly because of it—forcing teams to maintain ongoing collaboration rather than treating written specs as the end of communication.
